summary: Patrick McKenzie argues that directly marketing to online communities you don't belong to is almost always ineffective and often counterproductive. He compares the return on time from posting ads on forums (1 trial download per hour invested per month) against creating useful content like blog posts and resource pages (which generate 75-200x better returns). The most effective community-driven marketing comes from trusted members recommending your product organically, not from outsiders dropping in advertisements. He counsels that trust within a community is the key asset, and aggressively promoting your product to people who know you will destroy that trust for negligible financial gain. Instead, he recommends focusing on content creation and letting satisfied customers do the marketing for you.
key_points: Marketing directly to a community you're not part of risks violating social norms and is almost certainly a waste of time compared to alternatives
Content creation (blog posts, resource pages) generates 75-200x more trial downloads per hour invested than posting ads on community forums
The most effective community referral in his portfolio was one he didn't write — a trusted community member recommending his product carried far more weight than any outsider post could
Trust is the scarce resource in community marketing: if you have it, spending it on self-promotion destroys it; if you don't have it, building it takes too long relative to better alternatives
Your existing customers are your best marketing team because they are already trusted in communities you can't even access
money_quotes: It is ineffective to directly market to a community which you are not a part of, be that a forum community, a mailing list, or a Usenet group, regardless of what you are selling.

That community has its little social rituals, in-jokes, standards of acceptable behavior, shared history, friendships, rivalries, dramas, a whole tapestry of meaning for its members that you, the outsider, know nothing about.
The reason the link is so effective is because someone who was trusted in their community put their reputation on the line and said 'Hey, check out this site, it will help you educate your children'.
I'm trusted precisely because I have not been a self-promoter for the last three years, and I see no reason to throw that trust away now for a piddling amount of money.
Don't aggressively push your product at anybody who knows you and could possibly use it. That makes you into the Internet equivalent of the Mary Kay lady.
Customers are the first, last, and best marketing team you will ever have. They are already trusted in more communities than you can even conceive of.

Think of that: one hour per trial download per month. By comparison, there are many, many better ways to deploy one hour of my scarce time. One way would be to work at McDonalds, because a trial download is only worth about fifty cents to me.
key_insight: Community trust cannot be manufactured by outsiders, so software marketers should invest their time in creating useful content and serving customers well rather than posting ads in communities they don't belong to.
